name: mongodb
context: MongoDB ecosystem for document databases, mongosh scripting, aggregation, indexing, and ACID transactions
info: The MongoDB ecosystem covers secure connectivity, BSON document modeling, shell automation with mongosh, CRUD work through the Node.js driver, aggregation pipelines, query-plan-aware indexing, schema validation, and replica-set or sharded-cluster transactions. Agents use this tree when they need to inspect data, run maintenance scripts, build reporting pipelines, optimize query performance, or execute multi-step changes safely against MongoDB Atlas or self-managed deployments.
body:
# MongoDB Ecosystem

MongoDB is a document database built around BSON documents, flexible schemas, indexed queries, aggregation pipelines, and replica-set or sharded-cluster deployments. For an autonomous agent the practical jobs are consistent: connect safely, inspect data, read and write documents, summarize or reshape data with aggregation, tune performance with indexes, and only use multi-document transactions when the deployment and data model truly require them.

## Core Nodes

- **[[mongodb/auth]]** — connection strings, authentication mechanisms, TLS, Atlas connectivity, and secret handling
- **[[mongodb/mongosh]]** — interactive shell work and deterministic script execution with `--eval`, `--file`, and `--nodb`
- **[[mongodb/crud]]** — read and write documents with filters, projections, update operators, and bulk writes
- **[[mongodb/aggregation]]** — build pipelines with `$match`, `$group`, `$project`, `$lookup`, `$unwind`, `$sort`, and materialization stages
- **[[mongodb/indexes]]** — create, inspect, and troubleshoot single-field, compound, multikey, unique, TTL, and text indexes
- **[[mongodb/transactions]]** — wrap multiple operations in a session-backed ACID transaction
- **[[mongodb/schema-validation]]** — apply `$jsonSchema` validators and decide whether invalid writes should error or warn

## Execution Surface

MongoDB agents usually operate through two official surfaces:

- **Node.js driver** — `MongoClient`, `db()`, `collection()`, `find()`, `aggregate()`, `createIndex()`, `withTransaction()`
- **mongosh** — shell inspection, admin commands, and repeatable scripts that can run locally, in CI, or against Atlas

Use `MONGODB_URI` as the canonical secret. Prefer the narrowest-possible role, keep secrets out of logs, and load the specific workflow node instead of keeping all MongoDB behavior in one oversized playbook.

sections.title: Document model and BSON
sections.body: MongoDB stores BSON documents in collections. Documents can nest objects and arrays, and fields do not need to be identical across every document unless you enforce validation. This flexibility is powerful, but it shifts responsibility to the agent to use [[mongodb/schema-validation]] when shape consistency matters and [[mongodb/indexes]] when access patterns need predictable performance.

sections.title: How agents operate MongoDB
sections.body: Use [[mongodb/mongosh]] for direct inspection, administrative commands, and one-off or scripted shell tasks. Use [[mongodb/crud]], [[mongodb/aggregation]], [[mongodb/indexes]], and [[mongodb/transactions]] through the official Node.js driver when you need structured JSON input and output, retry logic, and repeatable automation.

sections.title: Deployment topologies
sections.body: MongoDB can run in Atlas, as a self-managed replica set, or as a sharded cluster. Topology affects which operations are available and how they behave. In particular, [[mongodb/transactions]] require a replica set or sharded cluster rather than a standalone `mongod`, and connection details in [[mongodb/auth]] differ between SRV-based Atlas URIs and explicit host lists.
